summary: Implemented landing.js for client-side project card grid rendering. Updated landing.html template to inject project data as window.__PROJECTS_DATA__ JSON with theme toggle, empty state, warning banner container, and skeleton card grid. Updated page.go handler to include lastUpdated (RFC3339) and warnings fields in project card data. Implemented card rendering with: project name, feature count, task stats, progress bar (green at >=80%), health status badge, relative time display, alphabetical sorting, skeleton loading state, error state (warning banners), empty state, dark mode toggle, and prefers-reduced-motion support.
keyDecisions: Data injected as window.__PROJECTS_DATA__ JSON variable (not application/json script tag) for direct JS consumption without JSON.parse step
IIFE pattern for landing.js to avoid global scope pollution
Skeleton cards render synchronously before requestAnimationFrame to prevent layout shift
Relative time formatter handles edge cases: future dates, invalid dates, fallback to absolute date format for >30 days
Warning banners rendered by JS from project warnings array rather than server-side to keep template simple
Dark mode toggle persists to localStorage and respects prefers-reduced-motion for all animations
Progress bar uses high-completion class (green fill) when completionPct >= 80 per ui-design.md spec
Added lastUpdated (RFC3339 format) and warnings fields to projectCard struct in page handler

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Renders one card per project from template-injected JSON data
Each card shows: project name, feature count, task completion progress bar, completion %, last updated (relative time), health status indicator
Loading state: skeleton cards while data loads
Error state: warning banner for projects with invalid paths
Empty state: No projects configured message when config has zero entries
Cards sorted alphabetically by project name
No layout shift during initial render

notes: JS file uses vanilla JS with document.createElement per task spec. No framework dependencies. All existing Go tests pass (69 handler tests, 154 total across all packages). Handler coverage improved from previous to 91.6%.
